nodes.name: Regular -ar verbs
nodes.pattern: stem + o/as/a/amos/áis/an
nodes.explanation: Regular -ar verbs conjugate by replacing -ar with person endings.

nodes.name: tener for physical states
nodes.pattern: tener + noun (hambre, sueño, miedo, frío, calor, sed)
nodes.explanation: Spanish uses tener (to have) for many physical states where English uses to be.
transferTraps: *Yo soy cansado* / *Yo soy hambre* — English 'to be + adjective' transfer

nodes.name: ser vs estar
nodes.pattern: ser = essence/identity; estar = state/location
nodes.explanation: Both translate as 'to be'; estar covers temporary states (estoy cansado), ser covers identity.
transferTraps: Single English 'to be' collapses the distinction

nodes.name: At the café
nodes.scenario: You walk into a warm little café in Seville at lunchtime. The friendly waiter wants to know if you're hungry or thirsty and what you'd like — order a meal using present-tense verbs and food words.

nodes.name: Making plans
nodes.scenario: A new friend calls you in the morning to plan the day. Talk about how you feel (tener/estar), what you want to do, and where you'd like to meet.

nodes.name: Preterite (regular verbs)
nodes.pattern: -ar: é/aste/ó/amos/asteis/aron · -er/-ir: í/iste/ió/imos/isteis/ieron
nodes.explanation: The preterite reports finished actions with a clear end point: yesterday, last week, in 2019.
transferTraps: Dropping the written accent that carries the person: *hable* instead of *hablé* (I spoke)

nodes.name: Ayer hablé con mi hermana
nodes.text: Ayer hablé con mi hermana durante una hora.
nodes.translation: Yesterday I spoke with my sister for an hour.

nodes.name: Comimos paella el domingo
nodes.text: El domingo comimos paella en casa de mis abuelos.
nodes.translation: On Sunday we ate paella at my grandparents' house.

nodes.name: Irregular preterites
nodes.pattern: ser/ir: fui, fuiste, fue · tener: tuve · hacer: hice · estar: estuve · poder: pude · decir: dije
nodes.explanation: A small group of very frequent verbs has its own preterite stem and unstressed endings (-e, -iste, -o).
transferTraps: Regularising the stem: *tení* or *tenió* instead of *tuve* / *tuvo*
Writing an accent on irregular endings: *tuvé*

nodes.name: Fui al médico
nodes.text: La semana pasada fui al médico y no pude ir a clase.
nodes.translation: Last week I went to the doctor and I couldn't go to class.

nodes.name: Imperfect tense
nodes.pattern: -ar: aba/abas/aba/ábamos/abais/aban · -er/-ir: ía/ías/ía/íamos/íais/ían (ser, ir, ver irregular)
nodes.explanation: The imperfect describes habits, ongoing scenes, age, weather and background in the past — no end point.
transferTraps: English 'used to' has no single Spanish word; learners overuse *solía* instead of the imperfect

nodes.name: Cuando era niño
nodes.text: Cuando era niño, vivía en un pueblo muy pequeño.
nodes.translation: When I was a child, I lived in a very small village.

nodes.name: Íbamos a la playa
nodes.text: Todos los veranos íbamos a la playa con mis primos.
nodes.translation: Every summer we went to the beach with my cousins.

nodes.name: Preterite vs imperfect
nodes.pattern: preterite = the event that moves the story · imperfect = the scene it happens in
nodes.explanation: Spanish forces a choice English does not: one past tense for completed events, another for description and habit.
transferTraps: A single English simple past covers both: *Cuando fui niño* instead of *Cuando era niño*
*Ayer era cansado* instead of *Ayer estaba cansado*

nodes.name: Leía cuando llamaron
nodes.text: Leía tranquilamente cuando alguien llamó a la puerta.
nodes.translation: I was reading quietly when someone knocked at the door.

nodes.name: Hacía frío, así que cerré
nodes.text: Hacía mucho frío, así que cerré la ventana.
nodes.translation: It was very cold, so I closed the window.

nodes.name: ir a + infinitive (near future)
nodes.pattern: voy/vas/va/vamos/vais/van + a + infinitive
nodes.explanation: The everyday way to talk about plans, equivalent to English 'going to'.
transferTraps: Dropping the obligatory 'a': *voy comer* instead of *voy a comer*

nodes.name: Voy a hablar con mi jefe
nodes.text: Mañana voy a hablar con mi jefe sobre el horario.
nodes.translation: Tomorrow I'm going to talk to my boss about the schedule.

nodes.name: Vamos a ver una película
nodes.text: Vamos a ver una película esta noche en casa.
nodes.translation: We're going to watch a film at home tonight.

nodes.name: Reflexive verbs
nodes.pattern: me/te/se/nos/os/se + conjugated verb (levantarse, ducharse, acostarse, llamarse)
nodes.explanation: When the subject acts on itself, Spanish marks it with a pronoun. Most daily-routine verbs are reflexive.
transferTraps: Possessives instead of the reflexive: *lavo mis manos* instead of *me lavo las manos*
Forgetting the pronoun entirely: *levanto a las siete*

nodes.name: Me levanto a las siete
nodes.text: Me levanto a las siete y me ducho antes de desayunar.
nodes.translation: I get up at seven and shower before having breakfast.

nodes.name: Nos acostamos tarde
nodes.text: Los domingos nos acostamos muy tarde.
nodes.translation: On Sundays we go to bed very late.

nodes.name: Present perfect (he hablado)
nodes.pattern: he/has/ha/hemos/habéis/han + participle (-ado, -ido; visto, hecho, dicho, escrito, puesto)
nodes.explanation: Used in Spain for finished actions inside the current time frame: hoy, esta semana, este año, alguna vez.
transferTraps: Splitting the two parts: *he no comido* instead of *no he comido*
Making the participle agree: *he comprada* instead of *he comprado*

nodes.name: He desayunado en casa
nodes.text: Esta mañana he desayunado en casa con mi pareja.
nodes.translation: This morning I had breakfast at home with my partner.

nodes.name: ¿Has estado en Granada?
nodes.translation: Have you ever been to Granada?

nodes.name: Direct object pronouns
nodes.pattern: me/te/lo/la/nos/os/los/las before the conjugated verb (or attached to an infinitive)
nodes.explanation: They replace the thing or person already mentioned, and they go in front of the verb.
transferTraps: English word order keeps the object after the verb: *Yo veo lo* instead of *Lo veo*

nodes.name: El libro lo compré ayer
nodes.text: ¿El libro? Lo compré ayer en el mercado.
nodes.translation: The book? I bought it yesterday at the market.

nodes.name: No las encuentro
nodes.text: Las llaves no las encuentro por ninguna parte.
nodes.translation: I can't find the keys anywhere.

nodes.name: Indirect object pronouns
nodes.pattern: me/te/le/nos/os/les + verb — answers 'to whom / for whom'
nodes.explanation: Spanish marks the receiver of the action with a pronoun even when the person is also named.
transferTraps: Omitting the pronoun when the person is named: *Escribí a mi profesora* instead of *Le escribí a mi profesora*

nodes.name: Le escribí un correo
nodes.text: Le escribí un correo a mi profesora esta mañana.
nodes.translation: I wrote an email to my teacher this morning.

nodes.name: Two pronouns together (se lo)
nodes.pattern: indirect before direct; le/les becomes se before lo/la/los/las
nodes.explanation: When both pronouns appear, the person comes first and le/les turns into se.
transferTraps: *Le lo di* instead of *Se lo di*
Reversing the order: *Lo me das*

nodes.name: Se lo di esta mañana
nodes.text: ¿El regalo para Ana? Se lo di esta mañana.
nodes.translation: The present for Ana? I gave it to her this morning.

nodes.name: gustar and similar verbs
nodes.pattern: (a mí) me gusta + singular / me gustan + plural — also encantar, interesar, doler, apetecer
nodes.explanation: The thing liked is the subject; the person is an indirect object, so the verb agrees with the thing.
transferTraps: Mapping English 'I like it' directly: *Yo gusto el café* instead of *Me gusta el café*
Not making the verb plural: *me gusta las películas*

nodes.name: Me gustan las películas
nodes.text: Me gustan mucho las películas españolas.

nodes.name: Le duele la cabeza
nodes.text: A mi hermano le duele la cabeza desde ayer.
nodes.translation: My brother has had a headache since yesterday.

nodes.name: por vs para
nodes.pattern: por = cause, duration, exchange, movement through · para = purpose, destination, deadline, recipient
nodes.explanation: One English word 'for' covers two Spanish prepositions with different jobs: reason (por) versus goal (para).
transferTraps: *Gracias para todo* instead of *Gracias por todo*
*Este regalo es por ti* when you mean *para ti*

nodes.name: Estudio para trabajar en Madrid
nodes.text: Estudio español para trabajar en Madrid el año que viene.
nodes.translation: I study Spanish in order to work in Madrid next year.

nodes.name: Pagué veinte euros por la camisa
nodes.text: Pagué veinte euros por esta camisa en las rebajas.
nodes.translation: I paid twenty euros for this shirt in the sales.

nodes.name: Comparatives
nodes.pattern: más/menos + adjective + que · tan + adjective + como · irregulars: mejor, peor, mayor, menor
nodes.explanation: Comparisons of superiority, inferiority and equality, plus four irregular forms that never take más.
transferTraps: *más bueno que* instead of *mejor que*
With numbers Spanish uses de: *más que veinte* instead of *más de veinte*

nodes.name: Este hotel es más barato
nodes.text: Este hotel es más barato que el otro, pero está más lejos del centro.
nodes.translation: This hotel is cheaper than the other one, but it is further from the centre.

nodes.name: Cocina tan bien como mi madre
nodes.text: Mi hermana cocina tan bien como mi madre.
nodes.translation: My sister cooks as well as my mother.

nodes.name: Superlatives
nodes.pattern: el/la/los/las + más + adjective (+ de) · absolute: -ísimo/a
nodes.explanation: The relative superlative compares within a group (with de, not en); -ísimo simply intensifies.
transferTraps: English 'in the city' becomes de la ciudad: *el más antiguo en la ciudad*

nodes.name: El más antiguo de la ciudad
nodes.text: Es el mercado más antiguo de la ciudad.
nodes.translation: It is the oldest market in the city.

nodes.name: El tren lleva retraso
nodes.text: El tren de las ocho lleva media hora de retraso.
nodes.translation: The eight o'clock train is half an hour late.

nodes.name: Pago con tarjeta
nodes.text: Prefiero pagar con tarjeta, no llevo efectivo.
nodes.translation: I prefer to pay by card, I don't have any cash on me.

nodes.name: ¿Qué me recomienda?
nodes.text: ¿Qué me recomienda de la carta de hoy?
nodes.translation: What do you recommend from today's menu?

nodes.name: Tenemos una reunión a las diez
nodes.text: Mañana tenemos una reunión a las diez con el equipo.
nodes.translation: Tomorrow we have a meeting at ten with the team.

nodes.name: Me llevo bien con mi cuñado
nodes.text: Me llevo muy bien con mi cuñado, salimos juntos los sábados.
nodes.translation: I get on very well with my brother-in-law, we go out together on Saturdays.

nodes.name: Tengo fiebre desde ayer
nodes.text: Tengo fiebre desde ayer y me duele todo el cuerpo.
nodes.translation: I have had a fever since yesterday and my whole body aches.

nodes.name: Paseo por el barrio
nodes.text: De vez en cuando paseo por el barrio después de cenar.
nodes.translation: From time to time I walk around the neighbourhood after dinner.

nodes.name: At the airport
nodes.scenario: You are checking in for a flight from Madrid to Bilbao. The agent asks about your luggage, offers you a window seat and warns you about a delay — handle it using travel words and near-future plans.

nodes.name: Buying a train ticket
nodes.scenario: At the ticket window of a Spanish station you buy a return ticket for tomorrow morning, ask what time the train leaves and whether it is direct.

nodes.name: Checking into a hotel
nodes.scenario: You arrive at a small hotel in Salamanca with a reservation for two nights. Confirm your booking, ask about breakfast times and Wi-Fi, and say what you are going to do this evening.

nodes.name: Buying clothes
nodes.scenario: In a clothes shop during the sales you ask for another size, try something on, compare two jackets and pay — the assistant asks cash or card.

nodes.name: A meal out
nodes.scenario: You have dinner in a busy neighbourhood place: ask what the server recommends, order a starter and a main dish, say you liked the food, then ask for the bill.

nodes.name: At the doctor's
nodes.scenario: You have had a fever and a sore throat for three days. Describe your symptoms, say when they started, and understand the prescription the doctor gives you.

nodes.name: Talking about your job
nodes.scenario: A new colleague asks what you do, what your working hours are like and what you are going to do after the meeting. Answer naturally and ask back.

nodes.name: Last weekend with the family
nodes.scenario: A friend asks what you did last weekend. Tell the story of a family lunch: what happened, what the weather was like and who was there — preterite for the events, imperfect for the scene.

nodes.name: Your daily routine
nodes.scenario: Your language partner wants to know how an ordinary weekday goes for you, from getting up to going to bed, and what you do only from time to time.

nodes.name: Present subjunctive forms
nodes.pattern: take the yo form, swap the vowel: hablar → hable · comer → coma · irregulars: sea, vaya, haya, sepa, dé, esté
nodes.explanation: The subjunctive is not a tense but a mood: it appears in the second clause when the first one is not stating a fact.
transferTraps: English has almost no subjunctive left, so learners keep the indicative: *Quiero que vienes*

nodes.name: Espero que tengas un buen día
nodes.text: Espero que tengas un buen día mañana.
nodes.translation: I hope you have a good day tomorrow.

nodes.name: Subjunctive after influence (querer que)
nodes.pattern: querer / pedir / recomendar / es importante + que + subjunctive (two different subjects)
nodes.explanation: When one person tries to influence another, the second verb goes into the subjunctive.
transferTraps: English uses an infinitive: 'I want you to come' → *Quiero tú venir* instead of *Quiero que vengas*

nodes.name: Quiero que vengas
nodes.text: Quiero que vengas a cenar con nosotros el viernes.
nodes.translation: I want you to come to dinner with us on Friday.

nodes.name: Es importante que descanses
nodes.text: Es importante que descanses antes del viaje.
nodes.translation: It is important that you rest before the trip.

nodes.name: Subjunctive after emotion and doubt
nodes.pattern: me alegro de que / me molesta que / no creo que / dudo que + subjunctive
nodes.explanation: Reactions and doubt take the subjunctive; a plain statement of belief (creo que) keeps the indicative.
transferTraps: Keeping the indicative after a negated belief: *No creo que es verdad* instead of *No creo que sea verdad*

nodes.name: No creo que sea buena idea
nodes.text: No creo que sea buena idea salir con esta lluvia.
nodes.translation: I don't think it's a good idea to go out in this rain.

nodes.name: Subjunctive with future time (cuando)
nodes.pattern: cuando / en cuanto / hasta que + subjunctive when the event has not happened yet
nodes.explanation: Habitual or past time clauses use the indicative; clauses pointing at an unrealised future use the subjunctive.
transferTraps: English present after 'when': *Cuando llego a Madrid, te llamo* for a future trip, instead of *Cuando llegue*

nodes.name: Cuando llegue, te llamo
nodes.text: Cuando llegue a Madrid, te llamo por teléfono.
nodes.translation: When I get to Madrid, I'll call you.

nodes.name: Conditional (would)
nodes.pattern: infinitive + ía/ías/ía/íamos/íais/ían · irregular stems: tendría, haría, podría, diría
nodes.explanation: Used for hypothetical situations, advice and polite requests — the courteous register in shops, hotels and offices.
transferTraps: Using the imperfect for 'would': *Me gustaba un café* instead of *Me gustaría un café*

nodes.name: Me gustaría reservar una mesa
nodes.text: Me gustaría reservar una mesa para cuatro personas.
nodes.translation: I would like to book a table for four people.

nodes.name: Yo en tu lugar hablaría con ella
nodes.text: Yo en tu lugar hablaría con ella antes de decidir.
nodes.translation: If I were you I would talk to her before deciding.

nodes.name: Connecting ideas
nodes.pattern: sin embargo · aunque · por lo tanto · a pesar de · en cambio · además
nodes.explanation: Connectors turn short A2 sentences into B1 paragraphs: contrast, concession, consequence and addition.
transferTraps: Using pero and sin embargo interchangeably in writing, or starting a sentence with *Pero sin embargo*

nodes.name: Sin embargo, decidimos quedarnos
nodes.text: Llovía sin parar; sin embargo, decidimos quedarnos un día más.
nodes.translation: It was raining non-stop; nevertheless, we decided to stay one more day.

nodes.name: Aunque no tengo mucho tiempo
nodes.text: Aunque no tengo mucho tiempo, intento estudiar todos los días.
nodes.translation: Although I don't have much time, I try to study every day.

nodes.name: Tengo una entrevista el martes
nodes.text: Tengo una entrevista el martes y me gustaría prepararla bien.
nodes.translation: I have an interview on Tuesday and I'd like to prepare for it well.

nodes.name: por un lado... por otro lado
nodes.text: por un lado... por otro lado
nodes.translation: on the one hand... on the other hand

nodes.name: No estoy del todo de acuerdo
nodes.text: En mi opinión, no estoy del todo de acuerdo con esa idea.
nodes.translation: In my opinion, I don't entirely agree with that idea.

nodes.name: A job interview
nodes.scenario: You are interviewed for a job in Valencia. Explain your experience in the past tenses, say what you would like to do in this role and ask about the contract and the hours.

nodes.name: Making a complaint
nodes.scenario: The room you booked is noisy and the heating does not work. Complain politely at reception using the conditional, explain what happened last night and ask them to change your room.

nodes.name: Giving advice to a friend
nodes.scenario: A friend is thinking about moving abroad for work. Give advice with 'te recomiendo que' and 'es importante que', say what you would do, and react to their doubts.

nodes.name: Discussing an issue
nodes.scenario: You and a Spanish friend discuss whether cars should be banned from the city centre. Give your opinion, concede a point with 'aunque', and disagree politely.

relations.note: English 'to be + adjective' produces *soy cansado*; Spanish uses tener + noun for these states.
English single 'to be' collapses the ser/estar distinction.
Both cover states; contrast them when either lapses.
embarazada = pregnant, not embarrassed (avergonzado/a).
Food semantic network: fruit → restaurant → ordering.
Lunch is the main meal, typically 14:00–15:30.
Once the present endings are automatic, the preterite endings follow the same person pattern.
The subjunctive reuses the yo-form stem the learner already produces fluently.
Conditional endings are the imperfect -ía endings on the full infinitive.
One English simple past has to be split into two Spanish tenses; learners default to the preterite for descriptions.
English blocks 'I have eaten yesterday'; peninsular Spanish freely says 'hoy he comido' but not 'ayer he comido'.
English 'I wash my hands' produces *lavo mis manos*; Spanish uses me lavo las manos.
English keeps the object pronoun after the verb, so learners say *Yo veo lo*.
'I like coffee' inverts in Spanish: the coffee is the subject — me gusta el café.
A single English 'for' hides the cause/purpose split behind por and para.
English 'more good' is blocked early, but Spanish learners still produce *más bueno* for mejor.
'I want you to come' has no infinitive equivalent: Quiero que vengas.
English keeps the indicative after 'I don't think that', Spanish switches mood.
English uses the present after 'when' for the future; Spanish needs the subjunctive.
English 'would' is a separate word, so learners look for an auxiliary instead of an ending.
estar constipado = to have a cold; constipated is estreñido.
éxito = success, not exit (salida).
sensible = sensitive; sensible in English is sensato.
The A1 restaurant words are the base for ordering, recommending and paying at A2.
Same shape of problem: one English word, two Spanish choices decided by meaning.
doler behaves exactly like gustar: me duele la cabeza.
tener frío/calor/fiebre — the A1 tener family carries straight into talking about health.
